records.ety: [F. danser, fr. OHG. dansōn to draw; akin to dinsan to draw, Goth. apinsan, and prob. from the same root (meaning to stretch) as E. thin. See Thin.]
records.morph: [imp. & p. p. Danced (?); p. pr. & vb. n. Dancing.]

defs: To move with measured steps, or to a musical accompaniment; to go through, either alone or in company with others, with a regulated succession of movements, (commonly) to the sound of music; to trip or leap rhythmically.
quotes: Jack shall pipe and Gill shall dance.
Good shepherd, what fair swain is this Which dances with your daughter?

defs: To move nimbly or merrily; to express pleasure by motion; to caper; to frisk; to skip about.
quotes: More dances my rapt heart Than when I first my wedded mistress saw.
Shadows in the glassy waters dance.
Where rivulets dance their wayward round.

defs: To cause to dance, or move nimbly or merrily about, or up and down; to dandle.
quotes: To dance our ringlets to the whistling wind.
Thy grandsire loved thee well; Many a time he danced thee on his knee.
A man of his place, and so near our favor, To dance attendance on their lordships' pleasure.

records.ety: [F. danse, of German origin. See Dance, v. i.]

defs: The leaping, tripping, or measured stepping of one who dances; an amusement, in which the movements of the persons are regulated by art, in figures and in accord with music.
A tune by which dancing is regulated, as the minuet, the waltz, the cotillion, etc.
quotes: Of remedies of love she knew parchance For of that art she couth the olde dance.
notes: ☞ The word dance was used ironically, by the older writers, of many proceedings besides dancing.
